questions.text: Design Model(s), as a part of the object-oriented design process, are:
questions.explanation: In object-oriented design, models include static structures (Class diagrams, Subsystem models) and dynamic behaviors (Sequence diagrams, State diagrams). Therefore, all the listed models are part of the design process.

questions.text: Which of the following statement(s) characterise(s) use cases?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use cases are derived from the clients' requests

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use cases specify the functionalities and the behaviour that the system which is being developed should have.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use cases are suitable for modelling interfaces between two systems.

questions.explanation: Use cases are capturing requirements from clients, describing the system's functional behavior, and can involve actors that represent other external systems interacting with the system under development.

questions.text: The description (flow of events) section in the UML Use case model helps in designing:
questions.explanation: The flow of events in a use case describes a sequence of interactions. This scenario text is the primary input used to design Sequence Diagrams, which visually represent the exchange of messages over time.

questions.text: Requirements elicitation is the process of deriving the system requirements through:
questions.explanation: Requirements elicitation involves various techniques to discover what stakeholders need. These include studying legacy systems, interviewing users, and analyzing the tasks they need to perform.

questions.text: Actors in a use case diagram can only represent human users.
questions.explanation: Actors in UML Use Case diagrams represent external entities that interact with the system. These entities can be humans, external hardware devices, or other software systems.

questions.text: _________________ use the software requirements document to understand what system is to be developed.
questions.explanation: System engineers use the SRS (Software Requirements Specification) document to understand the technical requirements and constraints of the system they are tasked with developing.

questions.text: _________________ means: can the requirements be implemented given available budget and technology?
questions.explanation: Realism (or feasibility) checks ensure that the requirements are actually achievable within the project's technical and financial constraints.

questions.text: Based on the grammatical approach, Nouns extracted from a scenario are useful to identify:
questions.explanation: In linguistic analysis for OOD (Abbot's approach), nouns and noun phrases correspond to potential classes and objects, while verbs correspond to operations (methods).

questions.text: Avoidance of the use of computer jargon is one of the guidelines for _________________
questions.explanation: When writing requirements, it is essential to use natural language that stakeholders (who may not be technical) can understand, hence jargon should be avoided.

questions.text: The next step after developing design models in the object-oriented design process is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: identifying the objects of the system

questions.explanation: In a standard OOD process, steps like designing architecture and identifying objects precede or are part of developing design models. The step following model development is typically 'Specifying Object Interfaces' or proceeding to 'Implementation'.

questions.text: UML state Models show how individual objects change their ________ in response to events.
questions.explanation: UML state machine diagrams (state models) depict the lifecycle of an object, showing how its internal state changes triggered by specific events.

questions.text: When generating system implementation using a graphical model, the model should be:
questions.explanation: To reliably generate or drive a system implementation from a model, the model must be both logically accurate (correct) and contain all necessary detail (complete).

questions.text: A creative activity in which you identify software components and their relationships based on customer's requirements is called:
questions.explanation: Software design is the process of defining the architecture, components, modules, and interfaces of a system to satisfy specified requirements.

questions.text: Which of the following diagram is time oriented?
questions.explanation: Sequence diagrams are interaction diagrams that show how objects interact in a specific time sequence, with time progressing down the page.

questions.text: Which of the following are common approaches for Object class identification?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Based on tangible things in the application domain

questions.explanation: Object identification typically utilizes grammatical analysis (finding nouns), domain-based analysis (identifying physical entities), or scenario analysis (mapping interactions).

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT true about "actors" in a use case diagram?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They interact with the described system

choices.label: B
choices.text: They represent roles of the users of the described system

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are always located within the described system

questions.explanation: Actors are external entities that interact with the system. In a Use Case diagram, they are drawn outside the system boundary.

questions.text: Structured object-oriented design processes involve developing a number of different system models. This may not be cost-effective for which systems' sizes?
questions.explanation: For small systems, the overhead of creating and maintaining many formal models often exceeds the benefits gained from the process.

questions.text: Which of the following UML diagrams is/are structural model(s)?
questions.explanation: Structural diagrams represent the static structure of a system. Class, Component, and Package diagrams are all examples of structural models.

questions.text: The process of gathering information about the proposed and existing systems is called:
questions.explanation: Both 'Requirements discovery' and 'Requirements elicitation' refer to the activity of collecting system requirements from stakeholders and existing documentation.

questions.text: The process of writing down the user and system requirements in a requirements document is called:
questions.explanation: Requirements specification involves documenting the elicited requirements in a formal and structured way, usually within a Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document.
